relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: At Shittim, desire and worship become entangled.
Israelites accept invitations to meals and bow before other gods.
The people yoke themselves to Baal of Peor, and covenant anger rises.
The ancient judgment turns toward public execution of leaders or offenders.
Moses orders the judges to kill those joined to Peor.
Zimri brings Cozbi openly before a grieving congregation.
Phinehas takes a spear and leaves communal process behind.
He kills both named people in the inner chamber, and the plague stops in the narrative.
The text counts twenty-four thousand dead.
Phinehas' zeal is credited with turning back destructive wrath.
A covenant of shalom is spoken over the aftermath of bloodshed.
Priestly continuity is attached to the zealot act in this ancient text.
The slain Israelite is named Zimri, a leader among Simeonites.
The slain Midianite woman is named Cozbi, daughter of a Midianite leader.
The command turns toward collective hostility.
Peor and Cozbi are used to frame Midian as an enemy people.

contemplative_note: Numbers 25 is received under moral protest. The chapter links zeal, killing, plague cessation, priesthood, and peace in a way that has repeatedly endangered real people. Its mystical companion refuses reenactment: shalom can only be received as the termination of violence, never as Divine permission to spear another human being.
